food_name: Sesame Oil (pure, toasted, dark — Chinese style)
food_subgroup: Vegetable oil — plant-based fat, finishing/flavouring oil

classification.processing_level: processed (sesame seeds roasted, then cold-pressed to extract oil)

serving.natural_language_portion: Sesame oil is used as a finishing/flavouring oil in tiny amounts — typically 1 teaspoon (4.5g) per dish. It is never used as a primary cooking oil due to its low smoke point and intense flavour.

nutrient_source.title: Sesame Oil (toasted, dark) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code EC000104; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code EC000104 — Sesame Oil, toasted.

gi_source: GI not applicable — sesame oil is pure fat with zero carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sesame oil has a favourable fat profile: 41.7g polyunsaturated fat (predominantly omega-6 linoleic acid) and 39.7g monounsaturated fat (oleic acid) per 100g. Relatively low in saturated fat (14.2g). Zero sodium. Sesame oil contains sesamin and sesamolin — lignans with demonstrated antihypertensive properties in clinical studies.
hypertension.guidance: Sesame oil is a heart-healthy finishing oil for patients with hypertension. The sesamin lignan content specifically has shown blood pressure-lowering effects in clinical trials (8–12 mmHg systolic reduction with regular 40ml/day consumption in one study). At the 1 teaspoon (4.5g) finishing dose typical in Singapore cooking, the absolute benefit is modest but the oil profile is favourable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate. Favourable polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fat profile. Sesamin may have modest insulin-sensitising properties through peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor (PPAR) activation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sesame oil is an acceptable and potentially beneficial oil for patients with type 2 diabetes at typical finishing doses. Its rich flavour means very little is needed, so caloric contribution is minimal (1 teaspoon = ~40 kcal). No glycaemic concerns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: The sesamol, sesamin, and sesamolin compounds in toasted sesame oil have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in laboratory models through NF-κB inhibition. High linoleic acid (omega-6) at very small doses does not meaningfully increase the omega-6:omega-3 imbalance typical of inflammatory diets.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sesame oil is an acceptable finishing oil for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Its anti-inflammatory lignans provide modest benefit. At the small doses used in Chinese cooking (1 teaspoon per dish), there are no significant omega-6 concerns. Prefer sesame oil over palm oil or partially hydrogenated fats as a finishing agent.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dark toasted sesame oil (Chinese-style zhima you) is the intensely flavoured, amber-brown oil pressed from roasted sesame seeds (Sesamum indicum). It has a rich, deep, nutty aroma that is the defining flavour note of many Chinese, Korean, and Japanese dishes. Due to its low smoke point and overwhelming flavour, it is used exclusively as a finishing/flavouring oil — drizzled over cooked dishes, added to marinades, dipping sauces, and dressings — never as a primary cooking oil. The fat profile is excellent: 41.7g polyunsaturated + 39.7g monounsaturated fat per 100g, only 14.2g saturated fat, zero trans fat. Contains bioactive lignans (sesamin, sesamolin) with antioxidant and antihypertensive properties. Sesame is a major allergen. Widely available in Singapore supermarkets (Yeo's, Kim Guan brand).

untoasted sesame oil: cold-pressed sesame oil — different flavour profile

confidence_reason: Exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (EC000104).
notes: Values per 100g dark toasted sesame oil. Sesame is a major allergen — check with patient before recommendation. The 1 teaspoon (4.5g) finishing dose provides approximately 40 kcal and contributes minimally to daily fat intake while providing significant flavour. Distinct from light/raw sesame oil (pressed from unroasted seeds) which has a lighter colour, milder flavour, and slightly different bioactive profile.
